content.text: The Two Planes Every Azure Service Exposes

Every Azure service exposes two distinct API surfaces that serve different purposes. The control plane manages the resource itself — creating it, configuring it, deleting it, assigning permissions to it. The data plane is the operational surface — reading and writing the actual data the resource stores or processes. These planes have different endpoints, different authentication audiences, and often different permission models.

Confusing the two is a common source of authentication errors: a token obtained for the ARM control plane (management.azure.com) will be rejected by a data plane endpoint like vault.azure.net or blob.core.windows.net, and vice versa.

attrs.alt: Control plane vs data plane: management operations vs data operations

content.text: All control plane requests go through management.azure.com — the ARM endpoint. RBAC roles (Owner, Contributor, Reader, and custom roles) control access. The token audience must be https://management.azure.com.
Create account, rotate keys, set CORS, change replication
Create vault, configure soft-delete, set firewall rules
Create account, add regions, configure consistency level
Create DB, set tier, configure firewall, manage replicas
Create cluster, add node pools, upgrade Kubernetes version
Create Function App, configure app settings, deploy code

# Control plane: create a storage account via ARM
az storage account create   --name mystorageaccount   --resource-group myRG   --location eastus   --sku Standard_LRS   --kind StorageV2

# Under the hood this sends:
# PUT https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mystorageaccount?api-version=2023-01-01
# Authorization: Bearer <token with aud=https://management.azure.com>

Data plane requests bypass ARM entirely and go directly to the service endpoint. Authentication can use an Azure AD token (with the service-specific audience), a connection string/access key, or a Shared Access Signature (SAS) token.
Upload blob, list containers, set blob tier
Get secret, set secret, list secret versions
Encrypt, decrypt, sign, verify, wrap key

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()

# Data plane: blob storage — token audience is https://storage.azure.com
blob_client = BlobServiceClient(
    account_url="https://mystorageaccount.blob.core.windows.net",
    credential=credential
)
container = blob_client.get_container_client("mycontainer")
container.upload_blob("hello.txt", b"Hello, Azure!", overwrite=True)

# Data plane: Key Vault — token audience is https://vault.azure.net
secret_client = SecretClient(
    vault_url="https://mykeyvault.vault.azure.net",
    credential=credential
)
secret = secret_client.get_secret("db-password")
print(f"Secret value: {secret.value}")
Why the Distinction Matters for Authentication

Azure AD issues tokens for a specific resource (audience). A token for management.azure.com cannot access Key Vault, and a token for vault.azure.net cannot access ARM. DefaultAzureCredential handles this transparently in SDKs by requesting the correct audience for each client class, but when obtaining tokens manually you must specify the right resource.
Manual Token for Control Plane vs Data Plane

# Token for ARM (control plane)
ARM_TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token   --resource https://management.azure.com   --query accessToken -o tsv)

# Use for ARM REST calls
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $ARM_TOKEN"   "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups?api-version=2021-04-01"   | jq '[.value[].name]'

# Token for Azure Storage (data plane)
STORAGE_TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token   --resource https://storage.azure.com   --query accessToken -o tsv)

# Use for blob data plane calls
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $STORAGE_TOKEN"   -H "x-ms-version: 2023-01-03"   -H "x-ms-date: $(date -u +%a,\ %d\ %b\ %Y\ %H:%M:%S\ GMT)"   "https://mystorageaccount.blob.core.windows.net/?comp=list"   | grep -o '<Name>[^<]*</Name>'

# Token for Key Vault (data plane)
KV_TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token   --resource https://vault.azure.net   --query accessToken -o tsv)

curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $KV_TOKEN"   "https://mykeyvault.vault.azure.net/secrets/mypassword?api-version=7.4"   | jq '.value'
RBAC Across Control and Data Planes

Control plane permissions and data plane permissions are separate in Azure RBAC. Having Contributor on a storage account (control plane role) does not grant access to read blobs (data plane permission). Data plane access requires roles with specific data actions.
Create/modify/delete the resource; cannot read data
Read resource configuration; cannot read data
Read, write, delete blobs; cannot modify account config
Read blobs only; cannot write or modify
Get, set, list, delete secrets in the vault
Full control over vault config and all secret/key/cert operations
Manage storage account; cannot read blob data

# Grant a service principal data-plane access to read blobs
az role assignment create   --assignee <service-principal-object-id>   --role "Storage Blob Data Reader"   --scope /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/myaccount

# Grant data-plane secret access to Key Vault
az role assignment create   --assignee <service-principal-object-id>   --role "Key Vault Secrets User"   --scope /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/myvault

# Verify what actions a role grants (look for DataActions vs Actions)
az role definition show   --name "Storage Blob Data Reader"   --query "{Actions:permissions[0].actions, DataActions:permissions[0].dataActions}"   --output json
DataActions in a role definition grant data plane permissions. Actions grant control plane permissions. A role with only Actions cannot access data even if the caller has Contributor on the resource.
Alternative Data Plane Authentication: Keys and SAS

Not all scenarios use Azure AD tokens for data plane access. Some services support access keys (symmetric keys that bypass RBAC entirely) and Shared Access Signatures (time-limited, scoped tokens derived from an access key or a user delegation key).

# List storage account access keys (control plane operation — requires ARM token)
az storage account keys list   --account-name mystorageaccount   --resource-group myRG   --query "[0].value" -o tsv

# Use the key directly for data plane access (bypasses RBAC)
KEY=$(az storage account keys list --account-name mystorageaccount --resource-group myRG --query "[0].value" -o tsv)

az storage blob list   --account-name mystorageaccount   --account-key "$KEY"   --container-name mycontainer   --output table
Generating a SAS Token for Data Plane Access

# Generate a SAS token scoped to a single container, valid for 1 hour
EXPIRY=$(date -u -d '1 hour' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ)
SAS=$(az storage container generate-sas   --account-name mystorageaccount   --name mycontainer   --permissions racwdl   --expiry "$EXPIRY"   --auth-mode login   --as-user   --output tsv)

# Use the SAS URL to access blobs without a Bearer token
curl -s "https://mystorageaccount.blob.core.windows.net/mycontainer?restype=container&comp=list&$SAS"   | grep -o '<Name>[^<]*</Name>'

# A user delegation SAS (--as-user --auth-mode login) is preferred over
# a key-based SAS because it is tied to the user's Azure AD identity
# and can be revoked by revoking the user's access
Azure AD (app roles) or API key
SDK Design: Management vs Client Libraries

Azure SDKs reflect the control/data plane split in their package structure. Management libraries target the control plane (ARM); client libraries (sometimes called data-plane SDKs) target the service-specific endpoint. You choose the right package based on whether you are managing infrastructure or working with data.
Python: Control Plane and Data Plane for the Same Resource

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.storage import StorageManagementClient        # control plane
from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient              # data plane

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
subscription_id = "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"

# --- Control Plane: create the storage account ---
mgmt_client = StorageManagementClient(credential, subscription_id)

account = mgmt_client.storage_accounts.begin_create(
    resource_group_name="myRG",
    account_name="mystorageaccount",
    parameters={
        "location": "eastus",
        "sku": {"name": "Standard_LRS"},
        "kind": "StorageV2",
    }
).result()  # wait for LRO to complete

print(f"Created: {account.name}")

# --- Data Plane: upload a blob ---
blob_client = BlobServiceClient(
    account_url=f"https://{account.name}.blob.core.windows.net",
    credential=credential          # DefaultAzureCredential now requests
                                   # storage.azure.com audience automatically
)

container = blob_client.get_container_client("uploads")
container.create_container(exist_ok=True)
container.upload_blob("readme.txt", b"Hello from Python SDK", overwrite=True)
print("Blob uploaded successfully")
JavaScript/TypeScript: Control Plane and Data Plane
Key Vault: Control Plane vs Data Plane in Practice

Key Vault is the most common place developers run into the control/data plane split. The management library creates and configures vaults; the secrets/keys/certificates libraries read and write vault content. Azure Policy can restrict vault configuration (control plane) independently of Key Vault access policies (data plane).
Creating a Vault and Reading a Secret

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.keyvault import KeyVaultManagementClient           # control plane
from azure.mgmt.keyvault.models import VaultProperties, Sku, AccessPolicyEntry, Permissions, SecretPermissions
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient                    # data plane

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
subscription_id = "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"
tenant_id = "your-tenant-id"
object_id = "your-object-id"  # identity that needs secret access

# --- Control Plane: create the vault ---
kv_mgmt = KeyVaultManagementClient(credential, subscription_id)

vault = kv_mgmt.vaults.begin_create_or_update(
    resource_group_name="myRG",
    vault_name="mykeyvault",
    parameters={
        "location": "eastus",
        "properties": VaultProperties(
            tenant_id=tenant_id,
            sku=Sku(name="standard", family="A"),
            access_policies=[
                AccessPolicyEntry(
                    tenant_id=tenant_id,
                    object_id=object_id,
                    permissions=Permissions(secrets=[SecretPermissions.GET, SecretPermissions.SET])
                )
            ]
        )
    }
).result()
print(f"Vault URI: {vault.properties.vault_uri}")

# --- Data Plane: read a secret ---
secret_client = SecretClient(
    vault_url=vault.properties.vault_uri,
    credential=credential          # requests vault.azure.net audience
)
secret_client.set_secret("db-password", "SuperSecret123!")
retrieved = secret_client.get_secret("db-password")
print(f"Secret: {retrieved.value}")
Azure RBAC vs Vault Access Policy

Key Vault supports two authorization models for data plane access. The legacy Access Policy model (per-vault, not RBAC-based) allows setting GET/SET/LIST permissions per identity directly in the vault. The recommended Azure RBAC model uses standard RBAC roles like Key Vault Secrets User and Key Vault Secrets Officer, enabling consistent management through the same RBAC system used for all other Azure resources.

# Enable Azure RBAC authorization (replaces Access Policy model) on a vault
az keyvault update   --name mykeyvault   --resource-group myRG   --enable-rbac-authorization true

# Now assign data-plane access via standard RBAC
az role assignment create   --assignee <object-id-or-upn>   --role "Key Vault Secrets User"   --scope /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/mykeyvault

# Verify the assignment
az role assignment list   --scope /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/mykeyvault   --query "[].{Assignee:principalName, Role:roleDefinitionName}"   --output table
Cosmos DB: Control Plane vs Data Plane

Cosmos DB's data plane accepts two authentication modes: Azure AD tokens (recommended) or master keys. The control plane creates and configures the database account; the data plane performs document operations.

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.cosmosdb import CosmosDBManagementClient          # control plane
from azure.cosmos import CosmosClient                              # data plane

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
subscription_id = "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"

# Control plane: check if the account exists and get its endpoint
mgmt = CosmosDBManagementClient(credential, subscription_id)
account = mgmt.database_accounts.get("myRG", "mycosmosaccount")
endpoint = account.document_endpoint
print(f"Cosmos DB endpoint: {endpoint}")

# Data plane with Azure AD (no master key needed)
cosmos_client = CosmosClient(url=endpoint, credential=credential)
# DefaultAzureCredential requests https://cosmos.azure.com audience

database = cosmos_client.get_database_client("mydb")
container = database.get_container_client("orders")

# Upsert a document
container.upsert_item({
    "id": "order-001",
    "customerId": "cust-123",
    "total": 149.99,
    "status": "pending"
})

# Query documents
for item in container.query_items(
    query="SELECT * FROM c WHERE c.status = @status",
    parameters=[{"name": "@status", "value": "pending"}],
    enable_cross_partition_query=True
):
    print(f"  Order: {item['id']}, Total: {item['total']}")
When authentication fails, the error message usually indicates which audience was expected. Learning to read these messages speeds up diagnosis significantly.
Token audience does not match the endpoint
AKV10000: Missing Bearer or PoP token
No token sent to Key Vault data plane
Use SecretClient with credential; don't use ARM token
Role lacks DataActions for the operation
Assign a data-plane role (e.g. Storage Blob Data Reader)
Azure AD token present but no data-plane role assigned
Add Storage Blob Data Contributor/Reader role assignment
The specified resource does not exist
Correct endpoint but wrong container/blob/secret name
Assign Contributor or a specific management role
Checking Both Plane Roles for a Principal

PRINCIPAL="user@example.com"  # or service principal app ID
RESOURCE_GROUP="myRG"
SUBSCRIPTION=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)

echo "=== Control Plane Roles (ARM) ==="
az role assignment list   --assignee "$PRINCIPAL"   --include-inherited   --query "[?scope contains '/resourceGroups/$RESOURCE_GROUP'].{Role:roleDefinitionName, Scope:scope}"   --output table

echo ""
echo "=== Data Plane Roles (Storage) ==="
az role assignment list   --assignee "$PRINCIPAL"   --include-inherited   --query "[?roleDefinitionName contains 'Data'].{Role:roleDefinitionName, Scope:scope}"   --output table

echo ""
echo "=== Data Plane Roles (Key Vault) ==="
az role assignment list   --assignee "$PRINCIPAL"   --include-inherited   --query "[?roleDefinitionName contains 'Key Vault'].{Role:roleDefinitionName, Scope:scope}"   --output table
Managed Identity: Cleanest Pattern for Data Plane Access

Managed identities are the recommended way for Azure services to access data planes without storing credentials. A VM, Function App, or AKS pod can use its managed identity to obtain tokens for storage, Key Vault, Cosmos DB, or any other service that supports Azure AD authentication — no connection strings in code or environment variables.

# Function App using managed identity to read from Key Vault
# No credentials in code — identity is assigned at the Function App level

import os
from azure.identity import ManagedIdentityCredential
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient
from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient

# In Azure runtime (Function App, VM, Container Apps, AKS with workload identity):
# ManagedIdentityCredential hits the IMDS endpoint (169.254.169.254) to get a token
credential = ManagedIdentityCredential()

# Key Vault data plane — reads API_KEY secret
kv_client = SecretClient(
    vault_url=os.environ["KEY_VAULT_URL"],
    credential=credential
)
api_key = kv_client.get_secret("api-key").value

# Storage data plane — writes to blob
blob_client = BlobServiceClient(
    account_url=os.environ["STORAGE_URL"],
    credential=credential
)
blob_client.get_blob_client("results", "output.json").upload_blob(
    '{"status": "done"}', overwrite=True
)

# Assign managed identity data-plane access
# Get the managed identity object ID of a Function App
IDENTITY_OID=$(az functionapp identity show   --name myFunctionApp   --resource-group myRG   --query principalId -o tsv)

# Grant Key Vault data-plane access
az role assignment create   --assignee "$IDENTITY_OID"   --role "Key Vault Secrets User"   --scope /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/mykeyvault

# Grant Storage data-plane access
az role assignment create   --assignee "$IDENTITY_OID"   --role "Storage Blob Data Contributor"   --scope /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/myaccount
SDK Design: Management vs Client Libraries

Azure SDKs reflect the control/data plane split in their package structure. Management libraries target the control plane (ARM); client libraries (sometimes called data-plane SDKs) target the service-specific endpoint. You choose the right package based on whether you are managing infrastructure or working with data.
Python: Control Plane and Data Plane for the Same Resource

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.storage import StorageManagementClient        # control plane
from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient              # data plane

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
subscription_id = "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"

# --- Control Plane: create the storage account ---
mgmt_client = StorageManagementClient(credential, subscription_id)

account = mgmt_client.storage_accounts.begin_create(
    resource_group_name="myRG",
    account_name="mystorageaccount",
    parameters={
        "location": "eastus",
        "sku": {"name": "Standard_LRS"},
        "kind": "StorageV2",
    }
).result()  # wait for LRO to complete

print(f"Created: {account.name}")

# --- Data Plane: upload a blob ---
blob_client = BlobServiceClient(
    account_url=f"https://{account.name}.blob.core.windows.net",
    credential=credential          # DefaultAzureCredential now requests
                                   # storage.azure.com audience automatically
)

container = blob_client.get_container_client("uploads")
container.create_container(exist_ok=True)
container.upload_blob("readme.txt", b"Hello from Python SDK", overwrite=True)
print("Blob uploaded successfully")

Key Vault is the most common place developers run into the control/data plane split. The management library creates and configures vaults; the secrets/keys/certificates libraries read and write vault content. Azure Policy can restrict vault configuration (control plane) independently of Key Vault access policies (data plane).

# --- Control Plane: create the vault ---

vault = kv_mgmt.vaults.begin_create_or_update(
    resource_group_name="myRG",
    vault_name="mykeyvault",
    parameters={

        "properties": VaultProperties(
            tenant_id=tenant_id,
            sku=Sku(name="standard", family="A"),
            access_policies=[
                AccessPolicyEntry(
                    tenant_id=tenant_id,
                    object_id=object_id,
                    permissions=Permissions(secrets=[SecretPermissions.GET, SecretPermissions.SET])
                )
            ]
        )
    }
).result()
print(f"Vault URI: {vault.properties.vault_uri}")

    vault_url=vault.properties.vault_uri,

# Now assign data-plane access via standard RBAC
az role assignment create   --assignee <object-id-or-upn>   --role "Key Vault Secrets User"   --scope /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/mykeyvault

# Verify the assignment

Cosmos DB's data plane accepts two authentication modes: Azure AD tokens (recommended) or master keys. The control plane creates and configures the database account; the data plane performs document operations.

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.cosmosdb import CosmosDBManagementClient          # control plane
from azure.cosmos import CosmosClient                              # data plane

subscription_id = "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"

# Control plane: check if the account exists and get its endpoint

account = mgmt.database_accounts.get("myRG", "mycosmosaccount")
endpoint = account.document_endpoint
print(f"Cosmos DB endpoint: {endpoint}")

# Data plane with Azure AD (no master key needed)
cosmos_client = CosmosClient(url=endpoint, credential=credential)
# DefaultAzureCredential requests https://cosmos.azure.com audience

database = cosmos_client.get_database_client("mydb")
container = database.get_container_client("orders")

# Upsert a document

    "id": "order-001",
    "customerId": "cust-123",
    "total": 149.99,
    "status": "pending"

# Query documents

    query="SELECT * FROM c WHERE c.status = @status",

RESOURCE_GROUP="myRG"
SUBSCRIPTION=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)

echo "=== Control Plane Roles (ARM) ==="
az role assignment list   --assignee "$PRINCIPAL"   --include-inherited   --query "[?scope contains '/resourceGroups/$RESOURCE_GROUP'].{Role:roleDefinitionName, Scope:scope}"   --output table

Managed Identity: Cleanest Pattern for Data Plane Access

Managed identities are the recommended way for Azure services to access data planes without storing credentials. A VM, Function App, or AKS pod can use its managed identity to obtain tokens for storage, Key Vault, Cosmos DB, or any other service that supports Azure AD authentication — no connection strings in code or environment variables.

# Function App using managed identity to read from Key Vault
# No credentials in code — identity is assigned at the Function App level

import os
from azure.identity import ManagedIdentityCredential
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient
from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient

# In Azure runtime (Function App, VM, Container Apps, AKS with workload identity):
# ManagedIdentityCredential hits the IMDS endpoint (169.254.169.254) to get a token
credential = ManagedIdentityCredential()

# Key Vault data plane — reads API_KEY secret
kv_client = SecretClient(
    vault_url=os.environ["KEY_VAULT_URL"],
    credential=credential
)
api_key = kv_client.get_secret("api-key").value

# Storage data plane — writes to blob
blob_client = BlobServiceClient(
    account_url=os.environ["STORAGE_URL"],
    credential=credential
)
blob_client.get_blob_client("results", "output.json").upload_blob(
    '{"status": "done"}', overwrite=True
)

# Assign managed identity data-plane access
# Get the managed identity object ID of a Function App
IDENTITY_OID=$(az functionapp identity show   --name myFunctionApp   --resource-group myRG   --query principalId -o tsv)

# Grant Key Vault data-plane access
az role assignment create   --assignee "$IDENTITY_OID"   --role "Key Vault Secrets User"   --scope /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/mykeyvault

# Grant Storage data-plane access
az role assignment create   --assignee "$IDENTITY_OID"   --role "Storage Blob Data Contributor"   --scope /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/myaccount

Control plane requests always go through management.azure.com, which is a global endpoint that cannot be blocked with service-specific firewalls. Data plane endpoints can be locked down with service firewalls, private endpoints, and virtual network service endpoints.
Blocks data access from non-allowed IPs; control plane still works
Blocks secret/key reads from non-allowed networks
Routes data traffic through VNet — no public data plane endpoint
Restricts data access to specific VNet subnets
Prevents ARM delete/modify; data access unaffected

# Lock down blob data plane to a specific VNet subnet
az storage account update   --name mystorageaccount   --resource-group myRG   --default-action Deny  # block all data plane by default

az storage account network-rule add   --account-name mystorageaccount   --resource-group myRG   --vnet-name myVNet   --subnet mySubnet

# Control plane (management.azure.com) is unaffected — you can still
# az storage account show, az storage account keys list, etc.
# Only blob/queue/table data access from outside mySubnet is blocked.

# Verify: test that data access is blocked from outside the VNet
az storage blob list   --account-name mystorageaccount   --container-name mycontainer   --auth-mode login 2>&1 | grep -i "firewall\|forbidden\|AuthorizationFailure"

The control plane (management.azure.com) manages what resources exist and how they are configured; the data plane (service-specific endpoints) is where applications read and write actual data. They use different Azure AD token audiences, and RBAC distinguishes between Actions (control plane) and DataActions (data plane) in role definitions. Understanding this boundary prevents the most common class of 401/403 errors in Azure applications: sending the wrong token to the wrong endpoint or assigning a role that covers only one plane when both are needed.
